skill.name: agent-tools
skill.description: Use the inference.sh belt CLI to discover, run, and manage hosted AI apps for image, video, text, search, audio, 3D, and social automation workflows.
skill.summary: Run hosted inference.sh AI apps from a local CLI workflow.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. Review found these are mainly markdown CLI examples, but the skill legitimately asks users to install and run a networked CLI that can upload local files, use API keys, and post to Twitter/X. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Pipe to Shell
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends installing the CLI with curl piped directly to sh. This is a real supply-chain risk because users execute remote code immediately, although the same section describes checksum and signature verification and provides a manual install alternative.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is explicit in install examples. It is risky but not clearly malicious because the skill names the expected installer domain and documents manual verification.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Can Upload Local Files and Run Third-Party Apps
medium_findings.description: The skill explains that local paths are automatically uploaded when used as app inputs. This is expected functionality, but users could disclose sensitive local files to a remote service if paths are chosen carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly states that local file paths are uploaded and shows absolute, relative, and home-directory path examples. This confirms a privacy risk in normal use.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling Through Environment Variables
medium_findings.description: The skill documents INFSH_API_KEY for CI/CD and troubleshooting. This is normal authentication guidance, but API keys are sensitive and can leak through shell history, logs, or shared environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact API key variable is documented, but there is no evidence the skill exfiltrates it. The risk is operational handling rather than malicious access.

medium_findings.title: Commands Can Modify External Social Accounts
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for posting tweets and running Twitter/X automation apps. This is declared functionality, but it can create public actions on connected accounts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The social automation commands are explicit and user-facing. The risk is confirmed, but the skill does not hide the behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The many external command detections are mostly fenced bash blocks and inline command names in documentation. They do not indicate hidden Ruby backtick execution in skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are markdown formatting around shell examples and command names. No Ruby source file or executable Ruby command substitution was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Metadata Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections map to YAML description text, markdown table rows, or checksum documentation. No implementation of MD5, SHA-1, or another weak algorithm was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive markdown or an API key table, and the only checksum reference uses SHA-256 for installer verification. No cryptographic implementation code is present in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Malicious Override Text
low_findings.description: The reviewed files do not contain suspicious instructions such as ignore previous instructions, system update, or claims that the skill is pre-approved.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of all five files found normal documentation headings and commands. No evidence found for prompt injection or instruction override content.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Piped to sh Installer
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote installation through curl piped to sh executes downloaded code immediately. Prefer the documented manual download and checksum verification path when possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command appears in multiple install sections. This is a known risky pattern even when used for a legitimate installer.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Local File Uploads
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI uploads files referenced by local paths for remote app execution. Users should review every path before running commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is documented directly and includes examples using absolute, relative, and home-directory paths. This creates a clear data disclosure surface.

content.user_title: Run Cloud AI Apps from the CLI
content.value_statement: Developers need a quick way to find and run hosted AI models without building infrastructure. This skill provides inference.sh CLI guidance for discovery, execution, authentication, file upload, and task tracking.
actual_capabilities: Guides installation and login for the inference.sh belt CLI.
Shows commands for browsing and searching public AI apps.
Provides examples for image, video, LLM, search, audio, 3D, and Twitter/X apps.
Explains inline JSON input, input files, version pinning, and no-wait task runs.
Documents local file upload behavior and task result retrieval.
limitations: Requires the external belt CLI and an inference.sh account.
Remote app execution can consume credits or account quota.
Local file inputs may upload files to inference.sh services.
App availability, model names, and provider behavior can change over time.

use_cases.title: Prototype AI Media Workflows
use_cases.description: Generate images, videos, audio, and 3D outputs from CLI commands during rapid product experiments.

use_cases.title: Run Hosted Models in Scripts
use_cases.description: Use sample inputs, inline JSON, task IDs, and saved results to automate model calls from shell workflows.

use_cases.title: Explore Available AI Apps
use_cases.description: Search the public app store, inspect schemas, and choose models before building a larger workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Find an App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the agent-tools skill to find inference.sh apps for image generation. Show the belt commands I should run and explain what each command returns.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-tools to run an inference.sh app for a short text prompt. Include a sample inline JSON input and the command to check the task result.

prompt_templates.title: Upload a Local File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-tools to prepare a local image-to-video run. Include the file path input, safety checks for the path, and the command to save the result.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Multi-Step Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-tools to design a CLI workflow that searches for an app, generates sample input, runs without waiting, checks status, and stores the output.

output_examples.input: Find a hosted image model and run one prompt from the command line.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns a short sequence of belt commands: search the app store, inspect the selected app, run it with inline input, and fetch the result.

output_examples.input: Create a video from a local image using inference.sh.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the upload step, asks the user to confirm the image path, then provides the app run command and task tracking command.
The assistant explains API key setup, recommends secret storage, and gives commands for non-interactive execution and saved output.

best_practices: Use manual install and checksum verification when you cannot review the remote installer.
Confirm every local file path before running commands that upload files.
Store INFSH_API_KEY in a secret manager for CI/CD workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installers to a shell in high-trust environments without review.
Do not pass sensitive documents as local file inputs unless the remote app needs them.
Do not automate Twitter/X actions without confirming account permissions and content.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps users run hosted inference.sh AI apps through the belt CLI.

faq.question: Does it require an external account?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow requires inference.sh CLI authentication or an API key.

faq.question: Can it run Claude models?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents OpenRouter examples that include Claude models.

faq.question: Does it upload local files?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation says local paths are uploaded when used as app inputs.

faq.question: Is the install command risk-free?
faq.answer: No. The curl to sh installer is convenient but should be reviewed or replaced with manual verification.

faq.question: Can it post to social media?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes Twitter/X app examples, so users should confirm account permissions first.
